title: Best Smart Power Strips in 2026: Surge Protection, Energy Monitoring & Voice Control Tested
description: Cut through the noise with our expert guide to the best smart power strips in 2026. We cover energy monitoring, surge protection, Alexa and Google Home compatibility, and how to choose the right one for your setup.

sections.heading: Smart Power Strip vs Smart Plug: Which Do You Need?
sections.body:
Finding the best smart power strip starts with understanding what separates it from a regular smart plug — and whether you actually need one. A smart plug converts a single dumb outlet into an app-controlled socket. That is fine if you want to automate a lamp or a coffee maker. But if you have a desk loaded with a monitor, a laptop charger, a desktop tower, and a couple of USB devices, a smart plug solves nothing. You still have a tangle of cords feeding into a dumb power strip.

A smart power strip gives you individually switchable outlets — typically four to six AC sockets plus two to four USB ports — all managed from a single app or voice command. You can cut power to your gaming PC without touching your router. You can schedule your home office gear to power down at midnight. Some models let you monitor energy draw per outlet, which is genuinely useful for identifying phantom-load offenders.

The trade-off is cost and complexity. Smart power strips run $35 to $90 depending on features, versus $10 to $15 for a basic smart plug. They also require a stable 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi connection, and the app experience varies wildly between brands. If you only need to automate one device, a smart plug is the smarter spend. If you are managing a cluster of devices — home office, entertainment center, gaming setup — a smart power strip pays for itself in convenience and energy savings within months.

One more consideration: not all smart power strips offer surge protection. If you are plugging in expensive electronics, surge protection rated at 1000 joules or higher is non-negotiable. Do not assume it is included just because the strip looks premium.

sections.heading: Best Smart Power Strips for Home Offices
sections.body:
The home office is where smart power strips earn their keep fastest. A typical setup draws power from a monitor, a desktop or laptop charger, external speakers, a desk lamp, a webcam hub, and maybe a space heater in winter. That is six to eight devices, and you want granular control over all of them without crawling under your desk.

What to prioritize for a home office smart power strip: individual outlet switching is essential, not optional. You want to be able to cut power to your monitor independently from your router — because rebooting your router while on a video call is a bad time. Look for at least four individually controlled AC outlets. USB-C charging ports are increasingly important; a 20W USB-C port means you can charge a modern phone or small tablet without occupying an AC outlet.

App reliability matters more in a home office than anywhere else. Brands that use their own cloud infrastructure sometimes suffer outages that leave you unable to remotely control devices. Look for strips that support local control via Matter or a local API as a fallback. As of 2026, Matter-compatible smart power strips are starting to appear, and they are worth the small price premium for the reliability gain.

Surge protection for a home office should be rated at a minimum of 1080 joules. Your monitor and laptop are the most vulnerable components. A strip with a connected equipment warranty — some manufacturers offer $50,000 to $150,000 in connected equipment coverage — gives you real peace of mind. Finally, cord length matters more than people admit. A six-foot cord is the practical minimum for most desk setups; three-foot cords are frustrating in real use.

sections.heading: Best Smart Power Strips with Energy Monitoring
sections.body:
Energy monitoring is the feature that separates genuinely useful smart power strips from ones that are just glorified remote-controlled outlets. Per-outlet energy monitoring tells you exactly how much power each device is drawing in real time, and tracks consumption over days, weeks, and months. That data is actionable: you can identify which devices are costing you the most on your electricity bill and make informed decisions about schedules and shutoffs.

Not all energy monitoring is created equal. Strip-level monitoring — where the strip reports total draw for all outlets combined — is better than nothing but limited. Per-outlet monitoring is what you actually want, and it commands a price premium. Expect to pay $55 or more for a strip with genuine per-outlet wattage and kilowatt-hour tracking.

Accuracy matters too. Some budget strips report wattage but are off by 10 to 15 percent compared to a calibrated kill-a-watt meter. For casual awareness that is fine. If you are trying to calculate actual electricity costs, look for strips that advertise plus or minus two percent accuracy — a spec that better-engineered models hit consistently.

Scheduling and automation amplify the value of energy monitoring. Once you know your home theater draws 40 watts in standby mode, you can set a schedule to cut power at midnight and restore it at 6 AM. Over a year, that kind of automation on a few devices can meaningfully reduce your electricity bill. Pair energy monitoring with voice assistant integration and you have a genuinely smart home energy management tool, not just a fancy power strip.

sections.heading: Best Budget Smart Power Strips Under $40
sections.body:
You do not need to spend $70 to get a functional smart power strip. The sub-$40 segment has improved significantly in 2026, with several strips offering individual outlet control, basic scheduling, and Alexa or Google Home support at a price that is easy to justify.

The honest trade-offs at this price point: per-outlet energy monitoring is almost always absent or stripped down to total-strip monitoring only. Surge protection ratings tend to be lower, typically in the 500 to 800 joule range rather than 1000-plus. Build quality is noticeably lighter — the housing feels less robust, and the outlet grip on plugs can loosen over time. USB ports at this price are usually USB-A only, and charging speeds are slower, often capped at 5W or 12W total across all USB ports.

What you do get: reliable Wi-Fi connectivity on 2.4 GHz, app control that works well enough for basic scheduling, and voice assistant compatibility that genuinely functions. For a bedroom nightstand setup, a kids' room, or a secondary living space where you want basic automation without a serious investment, a sub-$40 strip is entirely reasonable.

One thing to watch at this price: app longevity. Smaller brands sometimes discontinue their apps or cloud services within two to three years, leaving you with a dumb power strip. Stick with brands that have a track record — Kasa, Meross, and Govee have all maintained their ecosystems reliably. Avoid no-name strips with generic apps, even if the hardware looks identical to a name-brand product.

sections.heading: Compatibility: Alexa, Google Home, and HomeKit Support
sections.body:
Voice assistant compatibility is one of the most important specs to verify before buying, and it is also one of the most frequently misrepresented in product listings. Here is the reality of the landscape in 2026.

Alexa and Google Home compatibility is near-universal among established smart power strip brands. If a strip works with Alexa, it almost certainly works with Google Home too, since both ecosystems use similar cloud-to-cloud integration. Setup is straightforward: enable the brand's skill in the Alexa or Google Home app, link your account, and your outlets appear as individual devices you can control by name.

Apple HomeKit is a different story. HomeKit requires hardware-level certification, which adds cost and complexity. Genuine HomeKit-compatible smart power strips are rare and expensive. If you are in an Apple ecosystem and want to control your power strip via Siri or the Home app, your options are limited. The more practical path for Apple users is to use a HomeKit bridge device, or to wait for Matter-certified strips, which work natively with HomeKit through the Matter protocol.

Matter support is the most significant compatibility development of 2025 and 2026. Matter-certified smart power strips work across Alexa, Google Home, HomeKit, and SmartThings without cloud dependency, using local network communication instead. Response times are faster, reliability is higher, and you are not dependent on any single company's cloud staying online. If you are building a long-term smart home setup, prioritizing Matter compatibility now is the right call, even if it costs a bit more.

For most buyers, Alexa or Google Home compatibility is sufficient. Pick the ecosystem you already use, verify the strip supports it explicitly in the product listing, and you will be fine.

sections.heading: Safety Features to Look For: Surge Protection and Overload Shutoff
sections.body:
Safety is not a marketing checkbox — it is the reason you should care about specs that most buyers scroll past. Smart power strips handle multiple high-draw devices simultaneously, and a failure mode in one can cascade to damage everything connected.

Surge protection is the first thing to verify. The joule rating tells you how much energy the surge protector can absorb before it fails. A 500-joule rating is the bare minimum for non-critical devices. For a home office or entertainment center with expensive equipment, target 1000 joules or higher. Some premium strips offer 2000 to 4000 joules, which is meaningful protection against significant power events. Also check the clamping voltage — lower is better, with 400V or below being the standard for quality surge protectors. UL 1449 certification is the baseline safety standard to look for; do not buy a surge-protected strip that lacks it.

Overload protection is equally important. A smart power strip should have an internal circuit breaker that trips if total current draw exceeds the rated amperage — typically 15 amps for a standard US strip. Without this, overloading the strip is a fire risk. Check that the breaker is resettable, not a one-time fuse, so a trip does not mean replacing the entire strip.

Fireproof housing materials are worth noting. Quality strips use flame-retardant polycarbonate or ABS plastic that will not propagate a flame if a component fails internally. This spec is rarely advertised prominently but can be found in the product's safety certifications.

Finally, consider outlet spacing. Wide-spaced outlets that accommodate large transformer plugs without blocking adjacent sockets are a practical safety and usability feature. Cramped outlet spacing forces you to leave sockets empty, which wastes money and creates frustration.

sections.heading: How to Choose: A Decision Framework and Final Recommendations
sections.body:
Here is a straightforward framework for picking the right smart power strip without overthinking it.

First, count your devices and identify which ones need individual control. If you have fewer than three devices to automate, a smart plug or two is a better value. If you have four or more, a smart power strip is the right tool.

Second, decide whether energy monitoring matters to you. If you want to track and reduce electricity costs, budget for a strip with per-outlet monitoring and spend at least $55. If you just want scheduling and remote control, you can spend less.

Third, check your voice assistant ecosystem and verify compatibility explicitly. Do not assume — read the product listing carefully and check user reviews for reports of integration issues.

Fourth, assess your surge protection needs. Expensive electronics deserve a strip rated at 1000 joules or higher with UL 1449 certification. Budget setups with less critical gear can get by with lower-rated protection.

Fifth, think about future-proofing. If you are investing in a smart home that you want to last five-plus years, prioritize Matter compatibility now.

Concrete recommendations by use case: For a home office with a mix of expensive gear, prioritize surge protection above 1000 joules, per-outlet control, and a USB-C port. For a home entertainment center, focus on total outlet count, individual switching, and energy monitoring to tame standby drain. For a budget bedroom or secondary room setup, a sub-$40 strip from Kasa or Meross with basic scheduling and Alexa support is entirely sufficient. For Apple HomeKit users, wait for Matter-certified options or use a bridge — the native HomeKit strip market is too thin to recommend confidently. For anyone building a new smart home in 2026, Matter-compatible strips are the investment that ages best.

relatedKeywords: smart power strip with energy monitoring
smart power strip vs smart plug

faq.question: What is the difference between a smart power strip and a smart plug?
faq.answer: A smart plug converts one outlet into an app-controlled socket and is best for single devices. A smart power strip gives you multiple individually switchable outlets — typically four to six AC sockets plus USB ports — all managed from one app. If you need to automate a cluster of devices like a home office or entertainment center, a smart power strip is the right choice. Smart plugs are cheaper and simpler but do not scale well.

faq.question: Do smart power strips work with Alexa and Google Home?
faq.answer: Yes, the vast majority of smart power strips from established brands like Kasa, Meross, and Govee support both Alexa and Google Home. Setup involves enabling the brand's skill in your voice assistant app and linking your account. Each individual outlet typically appears as a separate device you can name and control by voice. Apple HomeKit support is much rarer and requires hardware certification, so options are limited if you are in the Apple ecosystem.

faq.question: Is surge protection included in all smart power strips?
faq.answer: No, and this is a critical point many buyers miss. Some smart power strips are marketed purely as smart control devices with no surge protection whatsoever. Always check the product listing for a joule rating and UL 1449 certification before buying. For home offices and entertainment centers with expensive electronics, look for a rating of at least 1000 joules. Budget strips may offer only 500 to 800 joules, which is adequate for less critical setups.

faq.question: What is per-outlet energy monitoring and do I need it?
faq.answer: Per-outlet energy monitoring tracks the wattage and kilowatt-hour consumption of each individual socket separately, rather than reporting a single total for the whole strip. It is useful if you want to identify which specific devices are costing you the most on your electricity bill and set targeted schedules to cut standby power. It is not essential for everyone, but if reducing energy costs is a priority, it is worth paying the premium — typically $55 or more — for a strip that includes it.

faq.question: What does Matter compatibility mean for smart power strips?
faq.answer: Matter is an open smart home standard that allows devices to work across Alexa, Google Home, Apple HomeKit, and SmartThings without relying on cloud servers. A Matter-compatible smart power strip communicates over your local network, which means faster response times, higher reliability, and no dependence on any single company keeping their cloud service running. Matter-certified smart power strips started appearing in 2025 and 2026 and are worth the small price premium if you are building a long-term smart home setup.

faq.question: How many outlets and USB ports do I actually need?
faq.answer: For a home office, four individually controlled AC outlets plus two USB ports is a practical minimum. For a full entertainment center with a TV, streaming device, soundbar, gaming console, and router, six AC outlets gives you more flexibility. USB-C ports are increasingly valuable in 2026 since they can charge modern phones, tablets, and even some laptops at useful speeds without occupying an AC outlet. Avoid strips where USB ports share a single low-wattage pool — look for at least 18W total USB output, with at least one USB-C port rated at 18W or higher.
